text: Partial, proxy evidence (not yet settled by a proving run): a leakage-controlled premise-retrieval simulation shows bridge structure is highly informative for premise selection, the core of retrieval-augmented proving. On held-out test theorems, ~49.5% are cross-domain (need an out-of-home-cluster premise); a within-domain retriever recovers only 61.9% of their premises, but expanding to the top-3 of 138 bridge-connected neighbour clusters lifts recall to 88.2% (top-1 74.0%, top-5 93.4%). So a theorem's out-of-domain premise demand concentrates into a handful of bridge-neighbour clusters - exactly what a bridge-aware retriever would exploit. This makes bridge-aware proving plausible but does NOT measure actual prover success rate or transfer; that remains the open payoff.

comments.control: Baseline control (e-0012): the 88.2% cross-domain recall is real but mostly a global-hub/popularity effect. At matched budget k, adding the biggest globally-cited clusters (popularity-k) recovers ~95-99% of the bridge-aware gain; per-home bridge ranking beats popularity by only <=1.2pt (consistently positive) and beats random by 11-32pt. So bridges aid premise selection chiefly by routing to a few integrative hubs, not via theorem-specific structure.
